audit_items.proof_trace: In the `send()` error path (lines 855-859), `close_and_free_remote(remote)` and `close_and_free_server(server)` are called but there is no `return` statement, unlike every other cleanup branch in the same function (lines 816-818, 826-828, 841-843 all return). Execution falls through to lines 869-870 where `setsockopt(server->fd, ...)` and `setsockopt(remote->fd, ...)` dereference both fully-freed structs — `ss_free(remote)` is confirmed in `free_remote()` at line 979.
audit_items.attack_scenario: An attacker connects through ss-local (SOCKS5 client), causes the remote shadowsocks server to send data, then forcefully closes their local TCP connection; when ss-local's `send(server->fd, ...)` back to the client fails with EPIPE/ECONNRESET (non-EAGAIN), the missing `return` causes `setsockopt(remote->fd, ...)` to dereference the freed `remote` struct, yielding a use-after-free exploitable for memory corruption or crash.
